unit.name: Where BCIs may go next: multimodality, inner speech, and ethics
unit.description: Surveys emerging BCI directions beyond the main system, including avatar control, near-usable speech BCIs, inner-speech decoding, and ethical concerns.
unit.summary: In the closing section, the speaker looks beyond the current prototype toward multimodal BCIs that decode not only phonemes but also speech-related gestures for richer outputs such as animated 3D avatars [ts=3903s][ts=3920s]. He then contrasts the lab's roughly 25% word error rate with a newer UC Davis system using four motor-cortex arrays that approaches near-zero word error after repeated training and is already used for everyday communication [ts=3969s][ts=3982s][ts=4010s]. The most ambitious research direction presented is inner-speech decoding, motivated by the gap between attempted-speech systems at roughly 60 to 70 words per minute and natural conversation near 150 words per minute [ts=4049s][ts=4051s]. Preliminary results suggest imagined speech or imagined mouth movement can be decoded above chance, but the speaker immediately raises privacy, memory-decoding, consent, and cognitive-enhancement questions that require scientists, engineers, and policymakers to deliberate together [ts=4098s][ts=4128s][ts=4200s][ts=4300s].

key_points.text: Multimodal BCIs may decode speech plus articulatory gestures to control outputs such as 3D avatars.
The lab's system is described as around 25% word error rate, while a newer UC Davis system is described as getting close to zero word error rate after continued training.
Attempted-speech systems are still slower than natural conversation, motivating inner-speech decoding.
Preliminary work suggests inner speech or imagined mouth movement can be decoded above chance.
Decoding inner speech raises ethical concerns about private thoughts, memory access, consent, and cognitive enhancement.

He then contrasts the lab's roughly 25% word error rate with a newer UC Davis system using four motor-cortex arrays that approaches near-zero word error after repeated training and is already used for everyday communication [ts=3969s][ts=3982s][ts=4010s]. The most ambitious research direction presented is inner-speech decoding, motivated by the gap between attempted-speech systems at roughly 60 to 70 words per minute and natural conversation near 150 words per minute [ts=4049s][ts=4051s]. Preliminary results suggest imagined speech or imagined mouth movement can be decoded above chance, but the speaker immediately raises privacy, memory-decoding, consent, and cognitive-enhancement questions that require scientists, engineers, and policymakers to deliberate together [ts=4098s][ts=4128s][ts=4200s][ts=4300s].

kp_catalog.name: Future BCIs, inner speech, and neuroethics
kp_catalog.description: Future BCI systems may become multimodal and less effortful, but they also raise privacy, consent, memory, and enhancement questions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because future BCIs, inner speech, and neuroethics clarifies future BCI systems may become multimodal and less effortful, but they also raise privacy, consent, memory, and enhancement questions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
